content.text: Chrome extensions in Manifest V3 use three primary script types that work together to provide browser functionality. Understanding their roles and communication patterns is essential for building extensions that integrate with automation tools like Playwright or Puppeteer alongside Claude agents.

attrs.alt: Chrome extension architecture (background, content, popup) diagram

content.text: In Manifest V3, the traditional persistent
— the extension's central event handler and coordinator.

: only runs when needed, then shuts down to save resources
- Cannot directly access the DOM of web pages
- Manages extension state, handles browser events, and coordinates communication between components

)
- Registers and manages context menus, commands, and shortcuts
and have direct access to the page's DOM.
- Injected into specific pages based on match patterns in

(their JavaScript doesn't conflict with page JavaScript)
- Can read and modify the DOM directly
- Can communicate with the service worker via
- Cannot access most Chrome APIs directly (limited to

- Read page content (for scraping, analysis, or augmentation)
- Modify page structure (inject UI elements, highlight content)
- Intercept and monitor user interactions
- Extract structured data from DOM elements
- Communicate scraped data back to the service worker
The popup is the UI that appears when users click the extension icon in the Chrome toolbar.
- Runs in its own isolated HTML page (

)
- Has access to the full Chrome API suite (like the service worker)
- Provides user interface and configuration options
- Communicates with the service worker and content scripts
- Shown/hidden based on user interaction (not persistent)

- Displays extension status and scraped data
- Provides configuration controls (URL patterns, extraction fields)
- Shows action buttons (trigger scrape, export data, clear cache)
- Renders results from content scripts via the service worker
- Handles user authentication and API key management
changes that the popup listens to

1. User clicks "Scrape" in popup
2. Popup sends message to service worker
3. Service worker gets active tab ID
4. Service worker sends message to content script in that tab
5. Content script extracts DOM data
6. Content script sends data back to service worker
7. Service worker stores data in chrome.storage
8. Popup listens to storage change and displays results
Manifest V3 (the current standard) introduced critical changes:
In the "Playwright / Puppeteer + Claude" context, Chrome extensions serve as the bridge between browser automation and AI agents:

via content scripts (DOM, screenshots, network data)
2.

and sends it to Claude via API
3.

User installs extension → clicks on product page
↓
Content script captures full DOM + visible text
↓
Service worker sends to Claude: "Extract all products from this page"
↓
Claude returns: { fields: ['name', 'price', 'rating'], selector: '.product-card' }
↓
Content script executes extraction using returned selector
↓
Results displayed in popup

This pattern lets Claude agents interact with real browsers through the extension's content scripts, while Playwright/Puppeteer handle the automation orchestration outside the browser. The extension provides a more lightweight, user-centric automation channel compared to full desktop browser automation.

Think of the extension as a three-layer system:
-

- Keep service workers lightweight and event-driven
- Use content scripts for all DOM operations (they have the only direct access)
- Use

for persistent state across component restarts
- Minimize message passing overhead (batch data, avoid frequent small messages)
- Test isolation: content scripts shouldn't interfere with page JavaScript
Understanding this architecture is fundamental for building extensions that safely and effectively integrate browser automation with AI agents like Claude.
Understanding the internals of chrome extension architecture (background, content, popup) helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with chrome extension architecture (background, content, popup) has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying chrome extension architecture (background, content, popup) in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

Chrome extension architecture (background, content, popup) is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
